text:
Fast fashion is honestly one of those topics where the more you learn the worse it gets. Like, you can buy a t-shirt for five euros now. Five euros! My grandmother talks about how clothes used to be expensive and you'd mend them and pass them down. Now people wear something once for an Instagram photo and throw it away. Speaking of which, influencers are a huge part of the problem because they do these massive haul videos where they show off like thirty items from Shein or Temu and it normalizes buying that much stuff.

But the environmental impact is insane. The fashion industry is one of the biggest polluters in the world — I think it's the second or third biggest, depending on how you measure it. All that synthetic fabric is basically plastic, so when you wash it, microplastics go into the water. And then the clothes end up in landfills. Have you seen those photos of used clothing mountains in Ghana? We ship our unwanted clothes to developing countries and pretend we're being charitable, but really we're just exporting our waste problem.

And the workers. People making these clothes earn almost nothing, work insane hours, in buildings that are sometimes literally unsafe — remember Rana Plaza in Bangladesh? Over a thousand people died. That was 2013 and things haven't really gotten that much better. Some brands do those sustainability reports now but most of it is greenwashing if you look closely.

The crazy thing is that even the cheap clothes aren't really saving consumers money in the long run because they fall apart so fast. You end up buying the same thing over and over. A well-made jacket that costs three times as much lasts ten times as long.

The pollution thing really bothers me though. Rivers near textile factories in countries like Bangladesh and Vietnam are literally different colors because of the dyes. And we're worried about plastic straws while ignoring the fashion industry? It doesn't make sense. Something needs to change but I don't really know what. Maybe better regulations? Or people could just buy less. But that's hard when everything is so cheap and everyone online is showing off new outfits every day.

answerKey.governingThought: Fast fashion's artificially low prices hide enormous costs — environmental destruction, worker exploitation, and a wasteful consumption cycle — that consumers, governments, and the industry must urgently address.
supportPillars: Environmental damage is massive and systemic: microplastics, textile waste exported to developing countries, river pollution from dyeing processes
Worker exploitation persists despite awareness — poverty wages, unsafe conditions, and inadequate reform since Rana Plaza
The low-price model is an illusion: poor-quality items cost more over time through constant replacement
Social media and influencer culture accelerate overconsumption by normalizing excessive buying
answerKey.structuralAssessment: The text has strong content but no organizing principle. It opens with price, jumps to influencers, then environmental impact, then workers, then back to consumer economics, then back to pollution. The environmental argument is split into two separate sections (microplastics/landfills early, river pollution late) instead of being consolidated. The influencer point is introduced early but never connected to a larger argument about systemic causes. The conclusion ('something needs to change but I don't know what') undermines the text by refusing to commit to a direction. Good evidence throughout but scattered without hierarchy.
answerKey.proposedRestructure: Lead: Fast fashion's cheap prices mask costs that consumers, workers, and the planet are paying. Pillar 1 (Environment): Consolidate all environmental damage — microplastics, landfill exports, river pollution — into one cohesive section showing systemic scale. Pillar 2 (Workers): Exploitation as the hidden subsidy behind low prices, from Rana Plaza to current conditions. Pillar 3 (Consumer illusion): The false economy of cheap clothing that falls apart. Pillar 4 (Systemic drivers): Influencer culture and social media as accelerants of a broken model. Conclusion: Regulation, transparency, and cultural shift are all needed — no single fix is sufficient.
